eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A project manager is leading a software implementation project. During sprint reviews, several team members struggle to configure test automation tools, causing repeated defects and delays. What should the project manager do first to support team performance by providing adequate training?

On a technology project, recurring quality issues reveal that several team members cannot effectively use the new automation framework. What is the first action the project manager should take to provide adequate training?

correct.text: Assess the specific competency gaps and determine which skills are required for the project work before selecting a training approach.
correct.why: PMI guidance emphasizes first determining required competencies and elements of training. A needs assessment ensures the training addresses the actual cause of poor performance.

distractors.text: Send the entire team to the same external certification course immediately so everyone receives identical training.
distractors.trap: Assumes formal certification is always the best first response without confirming the actual skill gap.

distractors.text: Replace the lowest performing team members with more experienced resources to avoid additional delays.
distractors.trap: Treats a development issue as a staffing removal issue instead of supporting team growth.

distractors.text: Escalate the issue to the sponsor and request that the sponsor decide which training vendor to use.
distractors.trap: Shifts the project manager's responsibility for team development to the sponsor too early.

distractors.text: Postpone all training decisions until the next phase so the current schedule baseline is not affected.
distractors.trap: Ignores that unresolved skill gaps continue to harm project performance.

explanation: The best first step is to identify the required competencies and compare them with current team capabilities. PMI guidance for this ECO task focuses on determining training needs before choosing training options, allocating resources, or measuring outcomes. Without a clear assessment of the skill gap, the project manager risks selecting training that is expensive, poorly targeted, or ineffective.
references: PMI Talent Triangle and PMP Examination Content Outline, People domain, task Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team and Development related guidance
PMI Knowledgebase, Build a Team and Training and Development topics

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A project team includes experienced field technicians who learn best by doing rather than through lengthy classroom sessions. The project requires them to operate a new inspection device within two weeks. Which training approach is most appropriate?

A project manager must quickly prepare hands-on technical staff to use new equipment. The staff have strong practical experience and respond poorly to theory-heavy instruction. What is the most suitable training option?

correct.text: Use structured on-the-job training with an experienced mentor so team members can practice the new task in the work setting.
correct.why: PMI recognizes on-the-job training, mentoring, and coaching as valid development approaches. This option aligns the training method with the team's practical learning needs and the short timeline.

distractors.text: Require all technicians to read the equipment manual independently and certify that they have reviewed it.
distractors.trap: Confuses passive information review with effective skill development for hands-on work.

distractors.text: Delay deployment until a university partner can provide a formal academic course on the device.
distractors.trap: Selects an overly formal and slow response when a project-focused practical method is available.

distractors.text: Ask the functional manager to evaluate the technicians at the end of the month and then recommend training.
distractors.trap: Defers action instead of addressing the immediate project need.

distractors.text: Assign only the most senior technician to use the equipment so no training is required for the rest of the team.
distractors.trap: Avoids building team capability and creates a single point of failure.

explanation: When the required capability is practical and time sensitive, structured on-the-job training supported by mentoring is often the best choice. PMI guidance explicitly includes formal and informal training methods such as on-the-job training, mentoring, and coaching. The key is selecting the training option that fits the need, the work context, and the learners, rather than assuming classroom instruction is always superior.
references: PMI Talent Triangle and PMP Examination Content Outline, People domain, task Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team performance and development guidance
PMI Knowledgebase, Training methods including classroom, online, computer-based, on-the-job training, mentoring, and coaching

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A project manager identifies that several business analysts lack the regulatory knowledge needed for an upcoming release. The team suggests sending them to an expensive external certification program. The training budget is limited and the release is in six weeks. What should the project manager do?

A team has a real skill gap that affects a near-term compliance release. An external certification course is available, but it consumes most of the training budget and may not align with the immediate work. What is the best action?

correct.text: Evaluate training options against the required competencies, schedule constraints, and available budget, then choose the most targeted approach that closes the immediate gap.
correct.why: PMI guidance stresses determining training options based on needs and allocating resources for training. The best decision balances competency needs, timing, and budget rather than defaulting to the most prestigious option.

distractors.text: Approve the certification program because external credentials always provide the highest long-term value.
distractors.trap: Assumes certification is inherently best even when it may not fit the immediate project need or budget.

distractors.text: Refuse all training because the project manager is accountable for protecting cost performance above team development.
distractors.trap: Treats training as optional even when capability gaps threaten delivery.

distractors.text: Move the analysts off the project and ask the sponsor for new resources with compliance experience.
distractors.trap: Ignores the possibility of developing existing team members through a right-sized intervention.

distractors.text: Submit a change request to increase the budget before analyzing any alternative training methods.
distractors.trap: Escalates cost before assessing lower-cost and better-targeted options.

explanation: The project manager should choose training based on the actual gap, constraints, and value to project outcomes. PMI guidance for this ECO task includes determining training options based on need and allocating resources for training. Certification can be appropriate in some cases, but it is not automatically the best choice if a shorter, less expensive, and more targeted intervention will adequately support team performance.
references: PMI Talent Triangle and PMP Examination Content Outline, People domain, task Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, resource and team development guidance
PMI Knowledgebase, determine training options based on training needs and allocate resources for training

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A project manager completed a training program to close a skill gap in requirements elicitation. Two iterations later, stakeholder rework remains high. Which actions should the project manager take next to support team performance?
After providing training to improve business analysis capability, a project manager still sees significant rework and unclear requirements. What should be done next?

correct.text: Measure whether the training addressed the intended competency gap by reviewing performance indicators such as rework trends and quality of elicitation outputs.
correct.why: PMI guidance includes measuring training outcomes. The project manager should verify whether the training produced the desired performance improvement.

correct.text: Reassess the root cause and adjust the development approach if the original training method did not match the actual need or learner context.
correct.why: If performance does not improve, the project manager should revisit the needs assessment and select a better targeted training or support approach.

distractors.text: Assume the training was effective because attendance was high and the team reported satisfaction with the sessions.
distractors.trap: Confuses training participation or satisfaction with demonstrated performance improvement.

distractors.text: Stop monitoring the issue because learning benefits often appear long after project closure.
distractors.trap: Avoids accountability for near-term project performance.

distractors.text: Immediately document the issue as a personal performance failure for all analysts without additional analysis.
distractors.trap: Jumps to blame rather than evaluating whether training and support were appropriate.

distractors.text: Replace the facilitator of the training and close the action as complete.
distractors.trap: Assumes the provider was the problem without validating the competency gap or training design.

explanation: Providing training is not the endpoint. PMI guidance for this task includes measuring outcomes, which means checking whether the desired capability improvement actually occurred and whether project performance improved. If the results are weak, the project manager should reassess the gap, confirm the root cause, and adapt the development approach instead of treating attendance or learner satisfaction as proof of success.
references: PMI Talent Triangle and PMP Examination Content Outline, People domain, task Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, team effectiveness and performance measurement guidance
PMI Knowledgebase, measure training outcomes and continuously assess and refresh team skills

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A project manager is leading a complex product implementation. Two senior specialists hold critical configuration knowledge, and the schedule shows they will roll off the project before user acceptance testing begins. Several newer team members will remain through closing. What should the project manager do first to support team performance by providing adequate training?

During a system deployment project, key experts are scheduled to leave before final testing, while less experienced team members will stay through completion. What is the best initial action to ensure the remaining team is adequately prepared?

correct.text: Create and execute a structured knowledge transfer plan with documented procedures, shadowing sessions, and handoff checkpoints before the specialists leave.
correct.why: This directly addresses the capability risk by preserving critical knowledge and preparing the remaining team members to perform the work effectively.

distractors.text: Escalate to the sponsor and request that the specialists remain assigned until the project is complete.
distractors.trap: This assumes resource extension is the primary solution instead of first mitigating the risk through training and transition planning.

distractors.text: Replace the newer team members with external consultants who already know the product configuration.
distractors.trap: This bypasses team development and may be unnecessary, costly, and disruptive.

distractors.text: Update the issue log to note the specialists' departure and monitor performance after they leave.
distractors.trap: This is passive risk tracking and does not proactively build team capability.

distractors.text: Ask the functional manager to evaluate the newer team members at the end of the project.
distractors.trap: End-of-project evaluation does not help the team perform the upcoming work.

explanation: When critical expertise is concentrated in a few individuals, the project manager should proactively preserve and transfer that knowledge before it is lost. A structured knowledge transfer approach supports team performance by reducing dependency on single experts, improving continuity, and enabling remaining team members to execute work with less disruption. This aligns with PMI guidance on developing the team, maintaining knowledge transfer, and ensuring the team has the capabilities needed to deliver project outcomes.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team and Stakeholders performance domain
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 9.4 Develop Team
PMP Exam Content Outline 2019, People domain, Support team performance by providing adequate training

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A project manager notices that a high-performing technical team is struggling because only one person is able to support both the integration environment and production release activities. The project manager wants to reduce dependency on a single specialist while improving resilience. What is the best action?
On a project with tight release windows, one team member is the only person who can perform two critical operational activities. What should the project manager do to best support team performance?

correct.text: Implement cross training so additional team members can perform both activities, reducing the single point of failure.
correct.why: Cross training broadens capability across the team, improves continuity, and directly supports sustained performance when critical work is concentrated in one person.

distractors.text: Keep the specialist assigned to both activities because specialization improves efficiency.
distractors.trap: This ignores the operational risk created by single-person dependency.

distractors.text: Move the activities to the risk register and accept the exposure because the specialist has been reliable.
distractors.trap: Risk acceptance is not appropriate when a practical team development response is available.

distractors.text: Transfer ownership of the activities to the operations manager after the next release.
distractors.trap: This changes accountability later but does not build current project team capability.

distractors.text: Delay the next release until a new expert is hired externally.
distractors.trap: This over-relies on procurement or staffing changes instead of developing the existing team.

explanation: A project manager should reduce concentration risk by developing redundancy in critical skills. Cross training is an effective way to strengthen team capability, improve flexibility, and support continuity if a key specialist becomes unavailable. PMI emphasizes developing the team and maintaining knowledge transfer so project work does not depend excessively on a single individual. This is a practical training response that improves both performance and resilience.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 9.4 Develop Team
PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team performance domain
PMP Exam Content Outline 2019, People domain, Support team performance by providing adequate training

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A project manager is leading a regulated healthcare project. A team member can perform the work technically, but new compliance rules require formal authorization before anyone can approve validation records. What should the project manager do?

During a compliance-sensitive project, a capable team member lacks the formal credential now required to perform approval activities under updated regulations. What is the best action by the project manager?

correct.text: Arrange for the required certification or authorization before assigning that approval responsibility to the team member.
correct.why: When formal credentials are required by policy or regulation, adequate training includes obtaining the necessary certification before the person performs restricted work.

distractors.text: Allow the team member to approve the records because the person already has strong practical experience.
distractors.trap: Experience does not substitute for a mandatory formal credential in a regulated setting.

distractors.text: Have the team member perform the approvals while a certified colleague reviews them afterward.
distractors.trap: This still assigns restricted work to someone who is not authorized to perform it.

distractors.text: Document the exception in the lessons learned register and continue to avoid schedule impact.
distractors.trap: Lessons learned are retrospective and do not resolve the immediate compliance gap.

distractors.text: Escalate to the steering committee to request a waiver from the regulation for this project.
distractors.trap: This assumes governance can override an external or mandatory compliance requirement.

explanation: In regulated environments, adequate training is not limited to practical capability. It may include formal certification, licensing, or authorization that is legally or procedurally required before certain work can be performed. The project manager should ensure the team is both capable and compliant before assigning responsibilities. This protects the project from quality, audit, and regulatory risk while supporting appropriate team performance.
references: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 9 Resource Management
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 9.4 Develop Team
PMP Exam Content Outline 2019, People domain, Support team performance by providing adequate training

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
subtopic: process alignment through role based training
prompts: A project manager inherits a globally distributed project team after a mid-project reorganization. Several work packages are delayed because handoffs between design, testing, and deployment are inconsistent across regions. The team members are individually competent, but they apply different local practices and terminology. Which two actions should the project manager take to support team performance?

After a reorganization, a distributed project team is missing deadlines because different regions use inconsistent methods for design, test, and deployment handoffs. Team members are skilled, but execution is not standardized. Which two actions would best improve performance through training?

correct.text: Provide role based training on the agreed project procedures, handoff criteria, and common terminology used across regions.
correct.why: This addresses inconsistency in execution by aligning team members on how project work is to be performed and transferred.

correct.text: Facilitate working sessions to standardize practices and embed them in team agreements and operating procedures.
correct.why: This reinforces training by converting shared understanding into explicit norms and repeatable ways of working.

distractors.text: Replace regional leads with a centralized coordinator who approves every handoff.
distractors.trap: This adds control overhead without developing team capability or fixing the underlying inconsistency.

distractors.text: Increase schedule contingency to absorb future handoff delays.
distractors.trap: This treats the symptom in the schedule rather than improving team performance.

distractors.text: Ask each region to continue using its preferred process as long as deliverables are eventually completed.
distractors.trap: This preserves variation that is already causing coordination failures.

distractors.text: Move all design, testing, and deployment work to the region with the strongest performance results.
distractors.trap: This may overload one group and ignores the need to build consistent capability across the team.

distractors.text: Record the differences in the lessons learned register and review them during project closing.
distractors.trap: This postpones corrective action until after the project has already suffered performance issues.

explanation: The problem is not a lack of individual competence but a lack of shared execution standards. In this situation, the project manager should use training to align the team on common procedures, terminology, and handoff expectations, then reinforce that alignment through explicit working agreements or operating procedures. PMI emphasizes that team performance depends not only on technical capability but also on coordinated collaboration, clarity of expectations, and shared ways of working.
references: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team performance domain
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 9.4 Develop Team
PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 9.5 Manage Team
